description: The §7.4 six-emoji SAS known-answer test. HKDF-SHA256 over the four pinned public keys (fixed order parent_ed ‖ parent_x ‖ child_ed ‖ child_x) with the canonical salt and the provisioning_nonce as info, truncated to 16 bytes, then mapped to six emojis via the pinned Matrix/Element 64-emoji table (first 36 bits, six 6-bit big-endian indices). Both pairing peers MUST derive these exact six emojis; substituting any of the four keys (incl. either X25519 key) changes them.

notes.bit_mapping: first 36 bits of the 16-byte output, big-endian (bit 0 = MSB of byte 0); six 6-bit indices into the 64-emoji table (ADR-038 D3); remaining bits unused
notes.table: Matrix/Element m.sas.v1 64-emoji list, index order 0–63, pinned in ADR-038 appendix and SasEmojiTable.EMOJIS
notes.independently_computed: the hkdf_output + indices were computed with a standalone Python hmac/HKDF implementation (reproduce command below), then frozen; the host test (SixEmojiSasRealKdfTest) reproduces them with the Bouncy Castle KDF, and RFC 5869 Test Case 1 independently anchors that KDF
notes.reproduce: python -c "import hmac,hashlib; s=b'openwarden-pair-v1'; ikm=bytes([1]*32+[2]*32+[3]*32+[4]*32); info=bytes([5]*32); prk=hmac.new(s,ikm,hashlib.sha256).digest(); okm=hmac.new(prk,info+b'\x01',hashlib.sha256).digest()[:16]; v=int.from_bytes(okm,'big'); print(okm.hex()); print([(v>>(128-6*(i+1)))&0x3f for i in range(6)])"  # -> 63b98004f20f7ed1c6f52a51d421740a / [24, 59, 38, 0, 1, 15]
notes.substitution: flipping one byte of any of the four keys, or of the nonce, changes the six emojis — the executable regression for the X25519-substitution MITM ADR-025 D2a closes

expect: DERIVED — both sides display [🍎, 🔔, ⌛, 🐶, 🐱, 🌷]; human Match → slice (e) pins; Mismatch → abort + MITM warning + burn nonce
